name: Yesa Reservoir and Sos del Rey Católico Loop
name_original: Embalse de Yesa - Sos del Rey Católico – circular desde Sangüesa
description: This challenging 51.2-mile road cycling route around Yesa Reservoir and through Sos del Rey Católico offers historical sights and 3543 feet
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Sangüesa/Zangoza traverse a diverse landscape, characterized by a blend of mountainous terrain, river valleys, and agricultural plains in Navarre, Spain. The region offers varied topography, from challenging climbs with significant elevation gains to more gentle routes along riverbanks. Natural features like the Yesa Reservoir and the Foz de Lumbier canyon provide scenic backdrops for rides. This varied environment caters to road cyclists seeking both demanding ascents and picturesque, relaxed journeys. Best road cycling routes around Sangüesa/Zangoza The most popular road cycling route is Yesa Reservoir and Sos del Rey Católico Loop, a 51.2 miles (82.4 km) trail that takes 3 hours 53 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain and scenic waterside views around the reservoir. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Sangüesa (Zangoza) – Royal Palace of Olite loop from Sangüesa / Zangoza, a difficult 73.4 miles (118.1 km) path. This route traverses varied terrain with substantial climbs, passing through historic areas. Local road cyclists also love the Castle of Xavier – Sangüesa Old Town loop from Sangüesa / Zangoza, a 26.5 miles (42.6 km) trail leading through historic landmarks like the Castle of Xavier and the medieval town center, often completed in about 2 hours 35 minutes. Road cycling around Sangüesa/Zangoza is defined by mountainous areas, river valleys, and agricultural lands. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The routes in Sangüesa/Zangoza are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 18 reviews. More than 600 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Sangüesa/Zangoza's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Sangüesa/Zangoza?
A: There are over 10 road cycling routes around Sangüesa/Zangoza, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels. The komoot community has explored these routes over 600 times.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Sangüesa/Zangoza?
A: The region offers a diverse landscape, blending mountainous areas with significant climbs, serene river valleys, and agricultural plains. You'll find routes that challenge with elevation gains and others that provide more relaxed rides through picturesque scenery.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, Sangüesa/Zangoza features several challenging routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38164314" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sangüesa (Zangoza) – Royal Palace of Olite loop from Sangüesa / Zangoza&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 118.1 km path with substantial climbs. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40313988" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruesta Castle – Ruesta Castle loop from Sangüesa / Zangoza&lt;/a&gt;, covering over 100 km with significant elevation.

Q: What historical sites can I visit while road cycling in the area?
A: The region is rich in history. You can cycle past the 10th-century &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/934414" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Xavier&lt;/a&gt;, the ancestral home of St. Francis Xavier. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38182607" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Xavier – Sangüesa Old Town loop from Sangüesa / Zangoza&lt;/a&gt; specifically incorporates this landmark. Additionally, the historic town of Sangüesa itself, with its Romanesque architecture like the Church of Santa María la Real, is a highlight.

Q: Are there any scenic natural features to see along the routes?
A: Absolutely. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e985017256/yesa-reservoir-and-sos-del-rey-catolico-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Yesa Reservoir and Sos del Rey Católico Loop&lt;/a&gt; offers stunning waterside views. The impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2933330" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Foz de Lumbier (Lumbier Gorge)&lt;/a&gt;, carved by the Irati River, is another significant natural attraction in the vicinity, with parts of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5087670" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Irati Greenway&lt;/a&gt; offering scenic cycling opportunities nearby.

Q: When is the best time of year for road cycling in Sangüesa/Zangoza?
A: The diverse terrain of Sangüesa/Zangoza makes it enjoyable across multiple seasons. Spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures for cycling, avoiding the peak summer heat, especially on routes with significant climbs. However, the region's varied landscapes can be appreciated year-round, depending on your preference for weather conditions.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about the routes in Sangüesa/Zangoza?
A: The road cycling routes in Sangüesa/Zangoza are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 18 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, which combines challenging ascents with scenic river and agricultural landscapes, and the opportunity to explore historical landmarks along the way.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many of the routes around Sangüesa/Zangoza are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e985017256/yesa-reservoir-and-sos-del-rey-catolico-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Yesa Reservoir and Sos del Rey Católico Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38182607" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Xavier – Sangüesa Old Town loop from Sangüesa / Zangoza&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Is there parking available for cyclists in Sangüesa/Zangoza?
A: Sangüesa/Zangoza, as a historic town and a hub for outdoor activities, typically offers various parking options within or near the town center. While specific cycling-dedicated parking isn't detailed, general public parking facilities are usually available for visitors looking to start their rides.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly road cycling routes?
A: While many routes in the region involve moderate to difficult climbs, the presence of river valleys and agricultural lands suggests that there are sections or shorter routes that could be more suitable for beginners or those seeking a less strenuous ride. Look for routes with minimal elevation gain if you prefer an easier experience.

Q: Can I find places to stop for refreshments or food along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes pass through or near historic towns and villages, including Sangüesa itself, which offer opportunities for refreshments. These towns often have cafes, restaurants, and shops where cyclists can refuel and rest during their ride.
tips: The road is quite busy, but still it deserves to be a highlight. At some point you can take a refreshing dip!

Despite its curious and striking name, this town is serious when it comes to history and places to visit. Here is the Palace of Sada where King Ferdinand II of Aragon, known as the "Catholic", was born, a place worth visiting to understand the history of Spain. It is also very beautiful to walk its streets and appreciate its stone houses and discover some corner with beautiful panoramic views.

Flanked by the Aragón River and a town on the Camino de Santiago pass for centuries, it is said that this town has a monument on every street and you can see for yourself. Among the sites, the convent of San Francisco de Asís and del Carmen and the palace of Ongay-Vallesantoro stand out among other historical sites from Roman times.

faqs.question: How challenging is this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is considered a difficult road ride, demanding a very good level of fitness. You should anticipate a significant elevation gain of around 1,080 meters over approximately 82.4 kilometers.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route largely consists of mostly well-paved surfaces that are easy to ride. However, be prepared for potential sections of unpaved or rough terrain, where you might need to dismount and push your bike. It incorporates national roads, regular roads, and some streets.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll experience diverse and historical landscapes. Highlights include cycling around the vast Yesa Reservoir, often called the "Sea of the Pyrenees." The route also passes through Sos del Rey Católico, a well-preserved medieval fortress village, and starts from Sangüesa (Zangoza), a historic town on the Camino de Santiago.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: While specific seasonal recommendations aren't provided, given the significant elevation and potential for unpaved sections, it's generally best to undertake such a challenging ride during milder weather conditions, avoiding extreme heat or cold, and ensuring roads are clear of snow or ice.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this circular cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route starts from Sangüesa (Zangoza). You should be able to find parking within the town to begin your ride.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route primarily on paved and some unpaved roads. While dogs are generally allowed in public spaces, bringing a dog on a challenging 82 km road bike tour is not typically recommended for their safety or comfort. It's best suited for cyclists only.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route. It utilizes public roads and trails.

faqs.question: Are there places to stop for food or water along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, you will pass through Sos del Rey Católico, a village with amenities where you can find cafes or places to refill water. Sangüesa (Zangoza), your starting point, also offers various services.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with any other notable trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route intersect with other paths. Notably, about 8% of the route follows the Aragonese Way, and smaller portions align with the Sendero Histórico: Zaragoza and the Via Sancti Martini - Chemin de Saragosse.

faqs.question: What's an important consideration for tackling this route?
faqs.answer: Be prepared for varied terrain. While mostly paved, some segments may be unpaved and difficult to ride. It's wise to have a bike suitable for such conditions or be ready to dismount and push your bike through rougher patches, especially if you're on a pure road bike.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this circular route?
faqs.answer: The estimated time to complete this challenging route is about 3 hours and 54 minutes, though this can vary significantly based on your fitness level and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.
